briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Vote on Bear Spray Sales Rules and Other Business Bylaws
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council considered several proposals affecting local businesses, including new rules to regulate the sale of bear spray, changes to plumbing permit fees, a renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area, and updated on-street parking rates. Council also moved forward with rezoning applications for new mixed-use developments and voted to pause FCM membership dues until Burnaby gets a seat on the Big City Mayors' Caucus.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business sells bear spray — new licensing rules may apply to you soon.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal bylaw — first, second, and third reading passed
Burnaby bear spray sales regulation bylaw — first, second, and third reading passed
Burnaby plumbing permit fee amendment bylaw — first, second, and third reading passed
Burnaby on-street public pay parking fee rate increase takes effect
Burnaby special outdoor events authorization period opens — Deer Lake, Central Park, Swangard
Burnaby special outdoor events authorization period closes

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to regulate bear spray sales and raise on-street parking fees
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council advanced several proposed bylaws — including new rules that would require businesses selling bear spray to meet licensing conditions, and a fee increase for on-street public pay parking. Council also moved forward with plans to renew the North Road Business Improvement Area for 2026–2031 and approved amended plumbing permit fees. These measures still require final bylaw adoption before they take effect.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray or operate near North Road, watch for final bylaw adoption — new rules will apply to your business licence.
briefs.industries: Retail, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Rewrite Introduces Five New Non-Residential Zones
briefs.summary: At its February 10, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council voted to move forward with proposed amendments to the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw that would create five new non-residential zoning districts — meaning new rules for what types of businesses and buildings are allowed in certain areas. These changes are part of the broader Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (a long-term city growth plan) and could affect where and how non-residential businesses can operate in Burnaby. The City Solicitor has been directed to draft the formal bylaw changes for final approval.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business location falls under the new zoning districts — contact Burnaby Planning at City Hall.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Metro Vancouver Construction Update for Burnaby Projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a presentation from Metro Vancouver on upcoming and active capital construction projects in Burnaby. Two senior engineers — one overseeing liquid waste (sewage and drainage) and one overseeing water services — will update Council on the status of major infrastructure work planned or underway in the city.
briefs.action_required: If construction near your business is a concern, watch for project details shared after the Feb 24 meeting.

key_dates.label: Metro Vancouver capital projects update presentation — Burnaby Council meeting

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to regulate bear spray sales and amend plumbing permit fees
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council voted on several proposals affecting local businesses. A proposed bylaw would add new rules for selling bear spray — meaning retailers may need to meet new licence conditions. Council also approved changes to plumbing permit fees and moved forward with renewing the North Road Business Improvement Area (a defined zone where businesses collectively fund local improvements) for 2026–2031.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray or do plumbing work in Burnaby, watch for the new bylaw details — your licence or permit fees may change.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal period begins

briefs.title: Council to vote on removing outdated building restrictions from Burnaby residential properties
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on removing old restrictive covenants — legal agreements registered on property titles that limit what can be built — from residential lots now zoned R1. These covenants were put in place under older zoning rules and currently block housing types, like duplexes or small multi-unit buildings, that are now fully allowed under Burnaby's updated zoning.
briefs.action_required: If your Burnaby R1-zoned property has a restrictive covenant, watch for this decision — it may remove barriers to redevelopment.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — discharge of restrictive covenants on R1 properties

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Lower Plumbing Permit Fees and Issue Refunds for Larger Developments
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would temporarily reduce plumbing permit fees for larger in-progress developments in Burnaby. A fee restructuring that took effect January 1, 2026 caught many contractors off guard, so the proposed fix would apply lower 2025-based rates (plus a 3.79% cost-of-living increase) to eligible projects with permits issued on or before June 30, 2026 — and issue refunds to those who already paid the higher rate.
briefs.action_required: If you pulled a plumbing permit after Jan 1, 2026 for a larger development, check if you qualify for a refund.

key_dates.label: Burnaby plumbing permit fee amendment — Council vote date
Burnaby plumbing permit reduced fee rate — deadline for eligible permits
Burnaby plumbing permit refund eligibility window closes — amendment adoption

briefs.title: Council to vote on renewing Burnaby North Road BIA for 2026–2031
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing the Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) — a designated zone where businesses pool funds for local marketing, safety, and street improvements — for a five-year term from April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2031. A proposed bylaw would set the total budget at $1,755,147.81, funded by a special tax on commercial and light-industrial property owners in the area, starting with a 35% increase in year one.
briefs.action_required: If you oppose the BIA renewal, submit a written Notice of Opposition to Legislative Services by March 30, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal — Notice of Opposition deadline
Burnaby North Road BIA renewal bylaw — Council final adoption vote
Burnaby North Road BIA Local Area Service Tax — levy takes effect
Burnaby property tax due date — includes BIA levy

briefs.industries: Retail, Food Service, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on new bear spray sales rules for retailers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed bylaw changes that would restrict who can sell bear spray in Burnaby and how it must be stored and tracked. If approved, only outdoor and sporting goods stores could sell bear spray, buyers would need to be 19 or older and show photo ID, and retailers would have to keep sales records for 12 months — with fines of up to $500 for breaking the rules.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, watch for the council decision — this could affect your licence and store practices.

key_dates.label: Burnaby bear spray sales bylaw — Council vote date

briefs.title: Council to Consider Natural Hazard Permit Program and $120K Lower Westridge Safety Study
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on launching a city-wide Natural Hazard Development Permit Area (DPA) program — a set of rules that would require property owners in flood, wildfire, or steep-slope zones to get special approval before building or renovating. Council will also consider spending $120,000 to hire experts to assess whether the Lower Westridge neighbourhood is too hazardous to allow the province's small-scale multi-unit housing rules to apply there.
briefs.action_required: If you own property in Lower Westridge or a flood, wildfire, or steep-slope area, watch this file closely.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Natural Hazard DPA and Lower Westridge Assessment
Burnaby Natural Hazard DPA project plan report to Council — mid-2026 target
Lower Westridge hazard assessment completion target — Q3 2026
Burnaby city-wide Natural Hazard DPA and Guidelines program — anticipated completion

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to vote on granting Telus utility access at Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on allowing Telus to access a strip of land at 3860 Sperling Avenue (the Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre site) to install and maintain telecom infrastructure. This access would be granted through a Statutory Right-of-Way — a legal agreement that gives a utility company the right to use a specific portion of a property — at no cost to the City.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on Feb 24, 2026 — this affects utility access near the Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Telus right-of-way at Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre

briefs.title: Council to vote on antenna size and height variances at SFU's South Sciences Building
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) — a special approval that lets a property owner bend specific zoning rules — for Simon Fraser University's South Sciences Building at 8888 University Drive. The permit would allow antennas on the building to be larger and taller than current city rules permit, supporting cell and emergency communication coverage for Burnaby Mountain and the wider Metro Vancouver area.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on Feb 24, 2026 — it could affect telecom infrastructure near your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby DVP #25-01 SFU antenna variances — council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on antenna size and height variances at SFU's Water Tower Building
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) — a special approval that lets a property bend specific zoning rules — for Simon Fraser University's Water Tower Building at 9001 University Crescent. The proposed permit would allow antennas on the building to be larger and taller than current city rules permit, supporting emergency and cellular communications for SFU, Burnaby Mountain, and the wider Metro Vancouver region.
briefs.action_required: If you are near SFU's Burnaby Mountain campus, monitor the February 24, 2026 council decision.

key_dates.label: Burnaby DVP #25-02 antenna variance — Council vote date

briefs.title: Council to vote on renewing commercial fleet vehicle parking permit at 7000 Lougheed Highway
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on renewing a Temporary Use Permit (TUP) — a time-limited permission to use land in a way not normally allowed under zoning rules — for commercial fleet vehicle parking and rentals at 7000 Lougheed Highway. If approved, the renewal would allow the existing operation to continue for another three years, serving industries like film production and courier services, with no public parking or public vehicle rentals permitted on site.
briefs.action_required: If this permit affects your fleet operations, attend the February 24, 2026 Council meeting to have your say.

key_dates.label: Burnaby TUP #26-01 renewal Council vote — public meeting date

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby paying $63,005 in FCM membership dues for 2026–2027
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a $63,005.33 payment to the Federation of Canadian Municipalities (FCM) — a national organization that gives cities a collective voice on federal policy. The funds are already included in Burnaby's 2026 operating budget, so no new spending is required if approved.
briefs.action_required: No action needed — this is a routine city membership fee with no direct impact on local businesses.

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby landmark sign — staff recommend living logo planting instead
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on what to do with the proposed 'City of Burnaby' landmark sign. Staff are recommending Option B: skip the $600,000 permanent sign for now and instead create a living Burnaby logo — made from plants — in a flower bed at Central Park. This can be done within the existing parks budget, with no new spending required.
briefs.action_required: Watch the February 24, 2026 council meeting to see which option is approved — it may affect tourism and public space near your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote on Landmark Sign initiative — council meeting

briefs.title: Council to vote on approving up to 84 outdoor amplified-sound events in Burnaby 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on allowing up to 84 special outdoor events with amplified sound at Deer Lake Park, Central Park, and Swangard Stadium between March 15 and November 1, 2026. Under the Burnaby Noise or Sound Abatement Bylaw — the city rule that limits loud sound in public spaces — Council must formally approve these events before they can go ahead; this year's proposed total is about 45% higher than last year.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near these venues, watch for the council decision — increased event traffic could affect your operations.

key_dates.label: Burnaby special outdoor events season start — authorization takes effect
Burnaby special outdoor events season end — authorization expires
Burnaby Council vote — special outdoor events 2026 authorization

briefs.title: Council to vote on renewing portable toilet rental contract with Jiffy John for $162,400
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on renewing its portable rental toilet contract with Jiffy John Rentals Ltd. for one year (April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2027) at an estimated cost of $162,400 including taxes. The toilets are used at city parks, festivals, and job sites on an as-needed basis, and the supplier is holding prices steady from the previous term.
briefs.action_required: If you supply portable sanitation services, watch this decision — it signals Burnaby's vendor is locked in through March 2027.

key_dates.label: Burnaby portable toilet contract renewal period starts
Burnaby Council vote on Jiffy John contract renewal — meeting date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to vote on $670,000 contract renewal for Burnaby security officer services
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing a one-year contract with Guardteck Security Corp to provide security officers at city facilities and special events, at an estimated cost of $670,000 including taxes. The company has held its prices steady for the renewal period, and no new bidding process is required because the original contract already included optional renewal years.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on Feb 24, 2026 — this could affect security service providers in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Guardteck security contract renewal decision

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Regulate Bear Spray Sales and Raise Parking Fees
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council advanced several proposals affecting local businesses. A proposed bylaw amendment would regulate the sale of bear spray — requiring sellers to meet new rules under the Business Licence Bylaw. Council also approved a rate increase for on-street public pay parking meters and moved forward with renewing the North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) for 2026–2031.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray or operate near North Road, review the new bylaw rules and BIA renewal terms now.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal period begins

briefs.title: Council to vote on Heritage Alteration Permit for Burnaby's Mathers House chimneys
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on approving a Heritage Alteration Permit (HAP) — a special permit required to make changes to a protected historic building — for the W.J. Mathers House at 6490 Deer Lake Avenue. The permit would cover the already-completed dismantling of three unstable chimneys above the roofline, plus planned future work to rebuild and stabilize them using internal structural bracing.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Mathers House HAP vote

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on raising on-street parking meter rates citywide
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposal to standardize on-street pay parking rates across the city to $4.00 per hour and $10.00 per day (at locations where all-day parking is allowed). If approved, this would affect metered parking zones in all four Town Centres and near several SkyTrain stations, and is expected to generate about $320,000 in additional revenue per year.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is near a metered zone — higher parking costs could affect customer visits.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote on on-street parking rate increase — public meeting

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on new energy-based EV charging fees
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on switching Burnaby's 73 public EV charging stations from time-based fees (charged by the hour) to energy-based fees (charged by the kilowatt-hour, or kWh — meaning you pay for the actual electricity used). A proposed bylaw would also add an idle fee to discourage drivers from leaving their fully charged vehicles plugged in during peak hours.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — new EV charging rates and idle fees could affect your business fleet or staff parking costs.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — proposed energy-based EV charging fees

briefs.title: Proposed: 20-Storey Mixed-Use Rental Tower at 5058 Imperial Street, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on advancing a rezoning application (REZ #25-13) that would allow a 20-storey building at 5058 Imperial Street in Burnaby. The proposed development would include 177 market rental apartments and three ground-floor commercial retail units, replacing an earlier approved plan for a smaller 6-storey building on the same vacant lot.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this rezoning could open nearby commercial leasing opportunities.

key_dates.label: REZ #25-13 Burnaby rezoning bylaw — First and Second Reading council meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Consider Major Rezoning for BLAKE Village at 6800 Lougheed Highway
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on advancing a rezoning application (REZ #25-18) that would update the master plan — the overall blueprint for how a large site gets developed — for BLAKE Village at 6800 Lougheed Highway. The proposed update would allow up to 21 mixed-use residential buildings with roughly 5,250 homes, new commercial spaces, and expanded public open space on a 19-acre former industrial site near the Bainbridge SkyTrain station.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this rezoning could affect nearby commercial tenants, contractors, and property owners.

key_dates.label: Burnaby BLAKE Village REZ #25-18 — First and Second Reading scheduled at future Council meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: 49-Storey Mixed-Use Tower at 2209–2225 Springer Avenue Up for Approval
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to move forward a rezoning application for 2209 and 2225 Springer Avenue in Burnaby. The proposal, submitted by Millenium Properties Ltd., would allow a 49-storey high-rise with up to 421 homes and street-level retail — replacing older industrial buildings on land currently zoned for general industrial use.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council vote — this rezoning could affect nearby property owners, tenants, and local businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ #25-20 Springer Avenue rezoning — Council vote at upcoming meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's 2026 Active Transportation Education and Promotion Programs
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of active transportation programs for 2026. The proposal would continue existing cycling programs — Go By Bike Week and Learn2Ride — and launch three new pilots: a Walking School Bus, a Walking Tuesday community event, and a one-week School Streets pop-up that temporarily closes streets in front of schools to cars.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on February 24, 2026 — these programs may affect school-area traffic and street access.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — 2026 Active Transportation programs approval

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's 2026 Environmental Stewardship Events Schedule
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a report outlining Burnaby's planned 2026 environmental stewardship events, including free and low-cost community activities like nature walks, fish releases, and the City Nature Challenge. The report also highlights the annual Environmental Awards program, which recognizes businesses and individuals for green achievements — nominations will be open to the public.
briefs.action_required: Consider nominating your business for Burnaby's Environmental Awards — watch the City website for the nomination window.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby's 2026 Climate Action and Environment Work Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to receive a report outlining what the City's Climate Action and Environment team accomplished in 2025 and what it plans to do in 2026. Key items on the proposed 2026 work plan include finalizing a new Streamside Development Permit to protect streams and wetlands, launching a public engagement process on raising construction waste diversion requirements, and beginning work on a Climate Adaptation Plan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the 2026 Construction and Demolition Waste Diversion Bylaw engagement process — it could raise compliance requirements for your projects.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Construction & Demolition Waste Diversion Bylaw — engagement results and proposed changes to Council
Burnaby Climate Adaptation Plan — preliminary framework to Environment Committee
BC Emergency and Disaster Management Act — provincial regulations anticipated to take effect

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Zero Emissions Building Retrofit Strategy Progress Update
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an update on the Zero Emissions Building Retrofit Strategy, which aims to help building owners switch to low-carbon heating and cooling systems like heat pumps. The report outlines what has been done so far — including simplified permits, new online resources, and zoning changes — and previews more actions planned for 2026 to make retrofits easier and more affordable for residents and businesses.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the 2026 launch of Burnaby's new unified Heating Permit — it could simplify your upgrade process.

key_dates.label: Burnaby unified Heating Permit launch — takes effect

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to consider referring Stoney Creek construction pollution concerns to staff
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on sending a community member's concerns about construction pollution in Stoney Creek to city staff for review. The proposal asks staff to look into stricter rules for development near the creek — including possibly pausing new development approvals in the Stoney Creek Watershed until stronger environmental protections are in place.
briefs.action_required: If you develop or build near Stoney Creek, watch for staff findings — new permit rules may follow.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Proposed Vacancy Management Program for 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Financial Management Committee is set to present Council with an overview of a proposed Vacancy Management Program. The program would create a formal process for deciding what happens when city staff positions become empty — with the goal of controlling costs, keeping city services running, and aligning spending with Council's priorities.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision — this could affect city services and staffing that impact your business.

briefs.title: Council to consider referring Burnaby Football Club's request for four new turf fields to staff
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on sending the Burnaby Football Club's request to city staff for review. The club is asking the city to add four new artificial turf fields at existing Burnaby locations to keep up with growing demand for sports field time.
briefs.action_required: Follow the city's sports field strategy process if your business or group depends on Burnaby field access.

briefs.title: Council to consider completed study on Burnaby's social infrastructure gaps and future needs
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a completed Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment (SINA) — a city-wide study that maps current and future gaps in community facilities like childcare centres, libraries, recreation facilities, healthcare, and schools across Burnaby's four quadrants. The study is not a new rule, but it will guide how the City plans and funds community amenities through 2050, including decisions tied to developer contributions and the upcoming Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for how SINA shapes future developer contribution rules — it may affect what amenities are required near your business.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to consider pausing Burnaby development approvals near Stoney Creek watershed
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on referring a request to city staff for further study. The request — made by the Stoney Creek Environment Committee — asks the City to create new protections for the Stoney Creek watershed through bylaws and rezoning rules, and to pause (temporarily stop) new development approvals in the area until those protections are in place. The committee says increased construction activity has led to more spill incidents and a drop in the local salmon population.
briefs.action_required: If you have development plans near Stoney Creek, monitor this staff review — a development pause could affect your timeline.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Stoney Creek watershed staff referral

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Rent Bank Statistics Report for 2021–2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a report on the Burnaby Rent Bank (BRB), which provides no-interest loans and grants to low- and moderate-income renters facing eviction or utility shut-off. The report shows demand is rising — applications jumped from 199 in 2022–23 to 306 in 2024–25 — and that more grants (which don't need to be repaid) than loans were issued in each of the last two years, signalling growing financial hardship among Burnaby renters.
briefs.action_required: If you rent out property in Burnaby, watch for the fall 2026 council report on continued BRB funding.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby Planning Committee's 2025 Review and 2026 Work Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a report outlining what the Planning and Development Committee accomplished in 2025 and what it plans to tackle in 2026. The work plan covers major upcoming changes that could affect property owners, developers, landlords, and tenants — including a new Zoning Bylaw, updated development fees, a Tenant Protection Bylaw, new tree rules, and streamlined building permit processes.
briefs.action_required: Track upcoming PDC meetings in spring 2026 — new bylaws on zoning, fees, and tenant rules are expected then.

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a report outlining key transportation projects completed in 2024–2025 and major initiatives planned for 2026. Upcoming projects include an electric bike share program, a School Street pilot (temporary car-free zones near schools), smart street lighting, curbside management strategy, and new pedestrian safety upgrades across the city.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Information Reports — February 24, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a section of information reports at its February 24, 2026 meeting. No body text or specific report details were provided, so the exact topics under consideration are not yet known. Business owners should monitor the full agenda for any items that may affect their operations.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Burnaby Council agenda for February 24, 2026 to see if any reports affect your business.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaws on Bear Spray Sales, BIA Renewal, and Fee Changes
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council gave first, second, and third reading — early approval steps before a bylaw becomes law — to several proposed bylaws. These include new rules to regulate the sale of bear spray by businesses, a renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area (a defined zone where businesses pay a levy to fund local improvements), and changes to plumbing permit fees and EV charging fees.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Text Amendment Advances to First and Second Reading
briefs.summary: At the February 24, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, two zoning bylaw amendments — Bylaw No. 14792 (a general text amendment) and Bylaw No. 14794 (covering 7305 & 7317 Buller Ave.) — were given first and second reading. This means the bylaws have cleared an early stage of the approval process but are not yet final law; further readings and a public hearing may still be required before they take effect.

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Five new zoning districts under Burnaby's 2050 land use plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw that would create five new zoning districts — defined areas that set rules for how land can be used and what can be built there. This change is designed to put the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (OCP) — the city's long-term blueprint for growth and land use — into effect.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the February 24, 2026 council meeting — new zoning districts could affect your property or development plans.

briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council gave first, second, and third reading — the formal approval steps before a bylaw becomes law — to several proposed bylaws. These include rules that would require businesses to follow new conditions when selling bear spray, a renewal of the Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area (a defined zone where local businesses pool funds for area improvements) for 2026–2031, and a minor increase to plumbing permit fees.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray or operate on North Road, watch for the final bylaw adoption — new rules may apply to your business.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area Bylaw Up for Council Approval
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would officially create the Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) — a defined zone where businesses pool resources to improve and promote the area. If approved, the BIA would run for five years (April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2031), with over $1.75 million in total funding paid out to the Burnaby North Road Business Association to cover streetscape improvements, graffiti removal, and business promotion.

Burnaby North Road BIA — proposed five-year term ends

briefs.industries: Retail, Professional Services, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Restrict Who Can Sell Bear Spray
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would limit bear spray sales in Burnaby to outdoor and sporting goods retailers only — no general retailers or other businesses could sell it. If approved, these authorized sellers would need to check ID, refuse sales to anyone under 19, lock up their stock, and keep detailed sales records for at least 12 months.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, watch for the council vote — this could restrict or end your ability to sell it.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to set $500 fines for businesses selling bear spray without ID checks
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would add $500 fines to Burnaby's Business Licence rules for retailers who sell bear spray without following safety rules. If approved, businesses could be fined for selling to anyone under 19, failing to check photo ID, not storing bear spray in a locked area, or not keeping proper sales records for at least 12 months.
briefs.action_required: Review your bear spray storage and sales record-keeping practices before this bylaw is approved.

key_dates.label: Burnaby bear spray sales bylaw — council vote date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to temporarily cut plumbing permit fees for most new developments
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would temporarily reduce plumbing permit fees for most new developments (excluding small-scale multi-unit housing and tenant improvements). If approved, the fee for a first fixture would drop from $121 to $70, and each additional fixture from $67 to $39 — for permits issued on or before June 30, 2026. Owners who already paid the higher fee between January 1 and March 10, 2026 could receive a refund for the difference.
briefs.action_required: If you pulled a plumbing permit in Burnaby between Jan 1–Mar 10, 2026, watch for a possible refund if this bylaw passes.

key_dates.label: Burnaby reduced plumbing permit fee window closes — permits must be issued by this date
Burnaby plumbing permit refund eligibility window opens — permits issued from this date
Burnaby plumbing permit refund eligibility window closes — last eligible permit date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Change How Advisory Body Rules Are Created
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change who controls the terms of reference — the written rules that guide how advisory bodies operate — for Burnaby's advisory committees. Under the proposal, the Mayor would direct how these rules are written, and both the Mayor, Council, or the Chief Administrative Officer could request updates. All changes would go through the Executive Committee before Council gives final approval.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if you sit on or work with a Burnaby advisory body.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Regulate Bear Spray Sales at Local Businesses
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council voted to direct the City Solicitor to bring forward bylaw amendments that would regulate the sale of bear spray by businesses in Burnaby. A proposed change to the Business Licence Bylaw — the city rule that governs who can sell what — would set new requirements for retailers who sell bear spray, with enforcement rules added to the Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the new bear spray sales bylaw — it could affect your business licence requirements.
briefs.industries: Retail, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning for 204 rental apartments near 10th Avenue
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw amendment (Bylaw No. 14780) that would rezone several properties on Newcombe St. and 10th Avenue in Burnaby. If approved, the rezoning would allow a developer to build two six-storey rental apartment buildings with a total of 204 purpose-built rental units and underground parking.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council adoption decision — this rezoning could affect nearby property values and rental supply.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 14780 — council adoption vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Correspondence Items from February 24, 2026 Meeting
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review and decide on correspondence items received for the February 24, 2026 meeting. The agenda notes that one specific item of correspondence was flagged for Council's decision, while all other correspondence was published in a separate public package. No further details about the content of the correspondence are provided in this agenda item.
briefs.action_required: Check the Burnaby Council Correspondence package dated Feb 24, 2026 for details that may affect your business.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Petition Opposing Proposed Drug-Permitted Shelter at 3020 Gilmore Diversion
briefs.summary: Council will consider a petition signed by 3,887 Burnaby residents opposing a proposed drug-permitted shelter at 3020 Gilmore Diversion. The group WeVote Burnaby Gilmore Shelter Watch is asking the City, BC Housing, and the Province to pause the project, hold full community consultations, and explore other locations and treatment-focused approaches instead.
briefs.action_required: Attend or monitor the February 24, 2026 Council meeting if this shelter could affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — proposed drug-permitted shelter petition considered

briefs.industries: Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Food Service

briefs.title: Council to vote on bear spray sales rules and shelter plan for 3020 Gilmore Diversion
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council voted on several items affecting local businesses and neighbourhoods. Two key items stand out: a proposed bylaw would regulate who can sell bear spray in Burnaby, adding new business licence requirements for retailers; and Council passed a motion asking BC Housing to submit a Good Neighbour Plan and an Operational Management Plan before the new shelter at 3020 Gilmore Diversion can open.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, watch for the new business licence bylaw — it may change how you can sell it.
briefs.industries: Retail, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to Vote on New Rules for Proposed BC Housing Shelter at 3020 Gilmore Diversion
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a motion that would require BC Housing to submit a Good Neighbour Plan and an Operational Management Plan before the proposed shelter at 3020 Gilmore Diversion can open. The motion also asks BC Housing to consider making the shelter 'high-barrier' — meaning residents would need to pass background checks and be free from alcohol and drug use.
briefs.action_required: Attend the February 24, 2026 Council meeting to follow or speak to this vote if your business is nearby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — 3020 Gilmore Diversion shelter motion

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
